notes_for_model: English is the base register and the lingua franca of the product — but English speakers are global: US, UK, Ireland, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, India, Nigeria, South Africa, Singapore, Philippines, plus an enormous body of L2 speakers from every country. Do NOT assume US idiom is universal: 'reach out', 'circle back', 'on the same page', 'rain check', 'touch base' read as American corporate noise to most non-US readers. Prefer plain forensic verbs ('he avoids', 'she repeats', 'they reframe'). Therapy vocabulary — gaslighting, narcissism, love-bombing, DARVO, stonewalling, contempt, projection, negging, breadcrumbing — is mainstream in English and may be used as forensic terminology when evidence supports it; do not use as a label without citing the specific signal. Avoid 'maybe / perhaps / could be / seems like / it might' filler — say what the evidence shows or admit insufficient evidence. Family structures vary widely: nuclear, blended, single-parent, chosen family, queer family, multigenerational immigrant households are all baseline normal — do not default to 'mom and dad' framing. Religious context appears as evidence (church, synagogue, mosque, temple references; prayer, sabbath, fast) — never moralize, never assume one tradition. Honorifics are minimal in English (Dr / Mr / Mrs / Ms / Mx / sir / ma'am / by-first-name) but their presence or absence IS evidence of distance and power; flag a sudden switch from first-name to title (or vice versa) as a meaningful tell. L2 speaker patterns (article omission, present-perfect drift, calques like 'how do you call this', 'I am here since Monday') are NOT evidence of dishonesty — flag them only as register/origin signal, never as deception. Gendered grammar is light but pronouns are politically charged in some contexts; if subject's pronouns are unclear use 'they' rather than guessing he/she. Brand mark 'tells' stays Latin lowercase; 'vøiddo' keeps the stroked-ø — never write 'voiddo' or 'Tells'.
